coredata.dc:title: Carbon nano dot decorated copper nanowires for SERS-Fluorescence dual-mode imaging/anti-microbial activity and enhanced angiogenic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Bodhisatwa
Dadhich, Prabhash
Pal, Pallabi
Thakur, Shaila
Neogi, Sudarshan
Dhara, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: Copper nanoparticles are explored significantly for their antimicrobial activity, especially for antibiotic-resistant strain infections. However, copper has severe toxic responses and mostly it is due to its generation capability of reactive oxygen species (ROS) molecules while interacting with in vitro or in vivo systems. In the current study, wire shaped copper nanostructures were synthesized via microwave irradiation with single step doping of carbon nanodots (CDs). The synthesized material (CuCs) was characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, TEM, FESEM, XRD, DLS, and XPS. The fluorescence spectroscopy, microscopy and Raman spectroscopy results suggested CuCs to work well as a bi-modal imaging nanoprobe (fluorescence/SERS). The cell culture studies prove significant cytocompatibility and ROS scavenging property of the samples with respect to control. Further, CuCs-gelatin nanocomposite thin films were prepared and implanted into rodent deep wound model. The histological study has showed enhanced angiogenesis in the subcutaneous region. The results were validated by immuno-histochemistry. The ROS scavenging and enhanced angiogenesis were validated via gene expression studies and a HIF-α induced enhanced angiogenesis mechanism was also proposed for better wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual mode imaging
Antibacterial activity
Free radical scavenging
Angiogenesis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) study
In vitro reactive oxygen species scavenging study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Fluorescence Spectra of CuCs (excitation at 360 nm); b) UV Vis Spectra of CuCs; c) FTIR Spectra of CuCs; d) DLS analysis of CuCs.
a) TEM bright field micrograph of CuCs (inset higher magnification); b) SAED pattern; c) FESEM image of CuCs; d) XRD results of CuCs.
XPS analysis of CuCs a) Survey Scan; b) S2p; c) Cu2p3; d) C1s; e) O1s.

a) Fluorescence Imaging of MSCs using CuCs Green Filter; b) Red Filter; c) Merged Image; (scale bar 50 μm) d) SERS imaging capability of CuCs (enhancement in absolute intensity due to SERS depicted by red arrow) e) Control cell imaging using CuCs and DAPI.

a) MTT assay (cell viability) results of CuCs at different concentrations; b) Hemolysis assay CuCs; c) RBC aggregation study; d) WBC aggregation study; e) Platelets aggregation study (p ≤ 0.05 is statistically significant, micrograph scale bar is 50 μm).
a) NBT assay; b) DCFH DA assay (n = 3, p ≤ 0.05 is statistically significant).
Plates for antimicrobial activity a) S aureus, b) E Coli; c) Graphical Comparison (n = 3, p ≤ 0.05 is statistically significant).
a) H&E staining 7d; b) H&E staining 15d; c) MT staining 7d, d) MT staining 15d (inset magnified images are provided, scale 50 μm for main images and 20 μm for the magnified images).
Immuno-histochemistry staining for CD-31 expression a) 7 day; b) 15 day (inset magnified images).

In vitro gene expression study for the genes a) GAPDH, b) HIF-α, c) PECAM, d) NAD-Quinone oxidoreductase, e) Glutathione Peroxidase f) Superoxide dismutase (n = 3, p ≤ 0.05 is statistically significant).
The proposed mechanism of ROS scavenging/enhanced angiogenesis in CuC.
Primer sequences of the genes and corresponding melting temperature.

head.ce:title: Carbon nano dot decorated copper nanowires for SERS-Fluorescence dual-mode imaging/anti-microbial activity and enhanced angiogenic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Rutgers the State University of New Jersey, NJ, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rutgers the State University of New Jersey

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Rutgers The State University of New Jersey, NJ, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. Department of Biomedical Engineering, Rutgers the State University of New Jersey, NJ, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rutgers the State University of New Jersey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, WB, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper nanoparticles are explored significantly for their antimicrobial activity, especially for antibiotic-resistant strain infections. However, copper has severe toxic responses and mostly it is due to its generation capability of reactive oxygen species (ROS) molecules while interacting with in vitro or in vivo systems. In the current study, wire shaped copper nanostructures were synthesized via microwave irradiation with single step doping of carbon nanodots (CDs). The synthesized material (CuCs) was characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, TEM, FESEM, XRD, DLS, and XPS. The fluorescence spectroscopy, microscopy and Raman spectroscopy results suggested CuCs to work well as a bi-modal imaging nanoprobe (fluorescence/SERS). The cell culture studies prove significant cytocompatibility and ROS scavenging property of the samples with respect to control. Further, CuCs-gelatin nanocomposite thin films were prepared and implanted into rodent deep wound model. The histological study has showed enhanced angiogenesis in the subcutaneous region. The results were validated by immuno-histochemistry. The ROS scavenging and enhanced angiogenesis were validated via gene expression studies and a HIF-α induced enhanced angiogenesis mechanism was also proposed for better wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Multidrug and antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections are one of the major challenges in current edge infection biology. With the random application of antibiotics and differential contact of bacteria and usual drugs in pathogenic environments like in hospitals has emerged the current microbiology research into a new endeavor with drug-resistant pathogens inclusive “superbugs”. This infection control scenario becomes even more challenging in case of the wound and post-implantation secondary infection management [1,2].

Copper is used from ancient age to make vessels to store water, especially in India. In Rome, the water pipeline was made from copper. They had faith in the bactericidal effect of copper ions. In the recent era following the historical tradition, researchers have explored copper nanoparticles (Cu-NP) for their bactericidal effects [3]. It has been observed that Cu-NP is significantly effective for both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [4]. However, the effectiveness of the Cu-NPs is strain dependent [5]. It also depends upon the surface chemistry and synthesis routes of the Cu-NPs [6].

Cu-NPs not only have been explored for checking its antimicrobial activity, its application is observed in the development of polymeric composite and also in metallic dental implants surface coatings [7]. The Cu-NPs are not only useful for because of its bactericidal effects but it has certain modulatory effects upon biochemical pathways also. It is observed Cu-NPs can affect Hypoxia Inducible Factor-α (HIF- α) in an up regulatory method [8,9]. Therefore, it has a modulatory effect on HIF- α pathway for angiogenesis. Cu-NPs enhance angiogenesis and differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) into endothelial cells [10]. It promotes translation of VEGF [11]. Therefore Cu-NPs could be excellent nanofiller for any graft-like material for deep wound healing [12]. Cu-NPs are observed also as a capable agent for imaging in SERS mode. So, wound healing can be modulated in situ via Cu-NP [13].

However, when the mechanism of bactericidal and antifungal was investigated for Cu-NPs, it has been observed that they generate a high amount of ROS which in turn cause bacterial protein and lipid peroxidation and DNA damage. This also has been observed to be the major mechanism via which Cu-NPs generate nanotoxicity into the in vitro and in vivo models [14,15]. Therefore, generation of excessive ROS could be a limitation for application of Cu-NPs. Recently it has been observed that the amount of ROS generation significantly depends upon the surface chemistry of the Cu-NPs [16,17]. Coatings of different materials such as biopolymers have been observed to be effective in reducing toxicity [18].

Carbon is observed to form a graphitic structure upon Cu-NPs and develop graphene oxide Cu-NP based SERS imaging probe [19]. Recently CDs have been observed to be effective in scavenging ROS from cellular micro environment [20,21]. It has been observed to scavenge ROS from both in vitro and in vivo [22]. It has been observed to react with the carbonaceous backbone and released as carbonate and bicarbonate ions. Considering all these facts from literature, in the current study, a simple one step method is developed to synthesize CD doped Cu nanowires (CuCs). Further, CuCs are explored for antimicrobial activity, fluorescence bioimaging, cyto, and hemocompatibility. Its ROS scavenging properties in the in vitro models are explored. Further, a gelatin-based nanocomposite is prepared out of it and explored for in vitro gene expression and in vivo deep wound model to check its angiogenesis enhancement capability for betterment of wound healing.
All materials are procured and used until mentioned. CuSO4 (5H2O), Sucrose and NaOH were procured from Merck. Deionized water (18.2 mΩcm−1) was utilized for experimentation.

A 0.5 (M) sucrose solution was prepared and alkalified using 0.1 (M) NaOH solution to pH 11. The solution was transferred to a domestic microwave (1000-Watt, 2.45 GHz). After 3 min the colorless solution turned into a yellowish suspension (CD suspension). The yellowish suspension in presence of UV light observed to show bright green fluorescence. The same suspension could stir at room temperature with a dilution in DI water of 1:10 and a 10 mM CuSO4 aqueous solution was added dropwise. The CuSO4 solution was originally pale blue, in turn after addition to CD suspension turn darker bluish green. Finally, a bluish green solution was obtained which was filtered using a 0.2 μm syringe filter. The pH of the solution was balanced to 7 by adding 0.1 (M) HCl, filtered, and freeze-dried to powder. A final suspension of the powder with a concentration of 100 μg. mL−1 was prepared in sterile 1X PBS for further applications (if not mentioned otherwise).

The synthesized CuCs were characterized with UV Vis spectroscopy (Varian Cary), fluorescence spectroscopy (PerkinElmer LS55), XPS (VG scientific ESCALAB MK-2, Mg Kα), HRTEM (JEOL, JEM-2100), FTIR (Thermo Nicolet, Nexus 8700), Raman spectroscopy (Jobin Yvon Horiba, T 64000), DLS (Malvern, Zetasizer), SEM (Carl Zeiss, EVO 60). The quantum yield (Qy) was measured by the protocol previously reported by taking acidified quinine sulfate as a control [23].

Wharton’s jelly-derived mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) were isolated under Institute Ethical Committee’s clearance (both Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, and Midnapore Medical College) and cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM-high glucose) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic (Hi Media, India). 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was conducted according to the protocol described by Mossman T (1983) [24]. The MTT assay was used to measure the mitochondrial activity, which is correlated with cell viability for both attached and poorly attached cells. Metabolically active cells can reduce the MTT tetrazolium salt to formazan crystals, while dead cells do not. Cells were grown on cell culture plates (24 well plates, poly-l-lysine coated, Nunc), with an initial seeding density of 104 cells/mL (n = 3), and the samples with different concentrations were added along with the media. The cells were incubated for 72 h. The culture media was discarded and the MTT salt solution (Invitrogen) was added to a concentration of 5 mg mL−1. The samples were incubated for 4 h. Finally, the cells were lysed by DMSO and the optical density was measured at 590 nm.

The bio-imaging study was conducted by growing cells on poly-l-lysine coated coverslips (Blue-Star) (104 cells/sample). The CuC mixed media was added (5 μg mL−1) to the cells and incubated for 12 h. The media was removed, and the coverslips were washed with PBS. The cells were counterstained with Ethidium Bromide (SRL Chemicals) for nucleus and imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The coverslips were mounted on glass slides (Hi Media) and imaged using both fluorescences as well as the DIC (differential interference contrast) mode. Two filters of excitation (592 nm bandpass 24 nm, and 470 nm bandpass 50 nm) and emission (675 nm bandpass 100 nm, and 540 nm bandpass 25 nm) was employed for capturing the fluorescence micrographs. All the micrographs were captured at 20X magnification.

Hemolysis assay was conducted in view of the material’s applicability to in vivo systems, like the optical imaging of blood vessels and blood-related sensing assays. Briefly, uncoagulated blood (3.8% citrate) was mixed with an equal volume of sterile normal saline. Different amounts of samples (5, 10 and 20 μg mL−1) were mixed with 1 mL of saline mixed blood and incubated for 60 min (n = 3) at 37 °C and controlled humidity. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged (400 g), and the optical densities were measured at 540 nm. Negative and positive controls were taken as normal saline and 1% Triton-X solution.

For the blood cell aggregation study, 5 mL of blood was collected from the subject (mixed with 3.8% Na-citrate 100 μL/mL of blood) and subdivided into two parts for red blood corpuscles (RBC) aggregation and for white blood corpuscles (WBC) and platelets adhesion studies [25,26]. For the RBC aggregation assay, 2 mL blood was centrifuged at 700 rpm for 10 min. The pellet was collected, and the pellet was mixed with normal saline in 1: 9 vol ratios. From that solution, 100 μL was taken and mixed with 600 μL of normal saline in the test tubes for each sample. The rest of the 1 mL tube was completed by adding the sample and saline to make up the volume up to 1 mL. The sample was incubated for 60 min, and afterward, 1 drop of the resultant solution was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope. For platelet and WBC aggregation assays, 4 mL uncoagulated blood was mixed with an equal volume of saline. This solution was mixed with HiSep™LSM-1077 (Hi Media) in a volume ratio of 3: 4. This mixture was centrifuged at 400 g for 30 min. The mixture during this treatment was separated into three distinct layers of platelet-rich plasma, WBC, and RBC. The platelet-rich plasma and WBC layers were isolated and mixed with the sample in a 9: 1 vol ratio. After incubation of 60 min, a drop was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope.

The CuCs synthesized are observed not only have imaging application but also therapeutic potential. The CDs are observed to be capable of scavenging free radicals from cells. The in vitro hydroxyl free radical scavenging property of the CuCs was verified via Di-Chloro Di-Hydrofuran Fluorescein Di-Acetate (DCFH-DA) assay [20]. Briefly, 10 000 cells per well were cultured on a poly-l-lysine-coated cell culture plate. Different concentrations of CuCs (10–30 μg mL−1) were added to the wells After 72 h of culture, the culture medium was discarded and washed with sterile PBS (n = 3). The cells were incubated with 1 mM methanolic DCFH-DA solution (Sigma-Aldrich) for 1 h and the fluorescence intensity was measured by excitation at 485 nm and emission at 530 nm. The inhibition of hydroxyl radical was measured with respect to the controls (without samples).

NBT (Nitro Blue Tetrazolium) assays in vitro via spectroscopy was performed by using the protocol described in our previous report, but with certain modifications [27]. Briefly, 6 mg mL−1 NBT (SRL Chemicals, Mumbai) solution was prepared in media and protected from light. MSC cultured in NBT mixed DMEM (Hi Media) were added to poly-l-lysine coated culture plates. Different concentrations of CuCs was added and kept for incubation for 24 h. For spectroscopy, the media was discarded, and the cells were lysed using DMSO and 1 M KOH solution. Finally, the optical density was measured at 610 nm.

The antimicrobial activity of CuCs was measured against S.aureus and E.Coli (Gram + ve and -ve) for applicability of the applicability of the material. 100 μg mL−1 stock CuC solutions were prepared. Whatman filter paper (BD-Biosciences) was cut into 8 mm circular discs and dip coated into the samples and vacuum dried at room temperature. For control standard antibiotic-antimycotic solution (HiMedia, 10000 units penicillin, 10 mg streptomycin and 25 μg amphotericin-B per mL of solution) was utilized. The antimicrobial activity was measured by measuring zone of inhibition in agar plates. The bacterial cell seeding concentration of S. aureus and E. coli were 1.47*107 and 1.1*107 CFU mL−1 respectively. The activity was measured in replica plates (n = 3).

To check this capability, square gelatin (porcine origin/Merck India) thin films (1 cm2 area and 0.2 mm thickness) were created via casting with CuC concentration (1.25 mg/gm of gelatin) and MSCs were cultured on them [Supplementary document] for cell compatibility study. The films were grafted on albino Wistar rats (n = 3, female) by creating deep wounds up to subcutaneous levels. After 7 and 15 days, the animals were sacrificed via euthanization. The sections were stained using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for wound healing and vascularization activity measurement. Masson Trichrome (MT) staining was conducted for understanding the collagen deposition and vascularization. Further expression of endothelial marker CD-31 was checked using immunofluorescence staining.

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining: The nuclei were stained with hematoxylin (3–4 min), rinsed in running tap water. Sections were further stained with eosin for 1 min followed by washing in running water to remove excess dye.

Masson trichrome (MT) staining: The hydrated tissue sections upon slides were treated with 10% Boulin solution at 56 °C and stain in Weigert’s iron hematoxylin working solution for 10 min. The slides were rinsed under running warm tap water for 10 min. The slides were further stained in Biebrich scarlet-acid fuchsin solution for 10–15 min. The slides were washed in distilled water. The slides color was differentiated in phosphomolybdic-phosphotungstic acid solution for 10–15 min until collagen is not stained red. The sections were transferred directly (without rinse) to aniline blue solution and stained for 5–10 min. The samples were rinsed briefly in distilled water and differentiated in 1% acetic acid solution for 2–5 min. Slides were washed in distilled water. Dehydration in this staining was performed very quickly with 95% ethyl alcohol, absolute ethyl alcohol (these steps will wipe off excess Biebrich scarlet-acid fuchsin staining) and cleared in xylene.

Immunohistochemistry protocol: The hydrated sections were incubated in 3% H2O2 solution prepared in methanol at room temperature for 10 min to block endogenous peroxidase activity. The sections were rinsed in 300 mL of PBS for 2 changes, 5 min each. Antigen retrieval step was conducted to unmask the antigenic epitope. The most commonly used antigen retrieval is a citrate buffer method. The slides were arranged in a staining container.300 mL of 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 6.0 was added into the staining container and incubated at 95–100 °C for 10 min (optimal incubation time should be determined by the user). The slides were taken out to cool for 20 min Slides were rinsed in 300 mL PBS for 2 changes, 5 min each followed by addition of 100 μL blocking buffer (e.g. 10% fetal bovine serum in PBS) onto the sections of the slides. The slides were incubated in a humidified chamber at room temperature for 1 h. After the incubation period, the blocking buffer was drained from the slides. 100 μL appropriately diluted primary antibody (Anti CD-31 Abcam, polyclonal to rat and rabbit dissolved in antibody dilution buffer, e.g. 0.5% bovine serum albumin in PBS) was added to the sections on the slides. The slides were incubated in a humidified chamber at room temperature for 1 h. The slides were washed in 300 mL PBS for 2 changes 5 min each. The slides were further incubated with 100 μL of fluorescent secondary antibody (Alexa Fluor-594 tagged, anti-human/rat developed in chicken, Thermo Fisher, 2 μg/mL) in dark humidified chambers for 30 min. It was washed with PBS twice and nucleus was counterstained with Hoechst-33342 fluorescent nucleus-specific dye (Merck, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) study
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidative stress marker gene expression down-regulation in presence of CuCs was studied via RT-PCR studies. Further, its angiogenesis activation property was also explored using gene expression. MSCs were seeded in the six-well plates and allowed to grow in cell culture media in presence of CuCs. RNA was isolated after 15d of culture and two consecutive days of UV exposure for 1 h. Different gene expression was studied according to the protocol provided in PCR kit (Genetix). GAPDH was kept as house-keeping gene and its expression was checked. The gene expression which was considered into account is glutathione-transferase, NAD-quinone-oxidoreductase, SOD, HIF-α, and PECAM (primer sequences and melting temperature is provided in Table 1 ). PCR amplification was performed for 35 cycles following the provided conditions (as reported by our previous studies): 94 °C (denaturation) for 30 s, followed by variable 1 (annealing) for 30 s, and 72 °C extension for 30 s and final extension at 72 °C for 10 min. PCR products were then analyzed by 1% agarose gel electrophoresis [28]. Primer sequences are mentioned in Table 1.

Statistical analysis was conducted by taking mean and standard deviation of samples run in triplicates (n = 3). The student’s t-test is conducted with p ≤ 0.05 to be statistically significant with respect to control wherever possible.

The physicochemical characterization of the CuCs was performed to check its properties like size, shape, bioconjugation etc. The fluorescence spectra were observed to leptokurtic in nature with multiple notches present in the spectra. The typical maxima were observed to be around 480 nm (Fig. 1 a). A notch around 545 nm was observed which was mostly explored during live cell imaging via fluorescence microscopy. The quantum yield with respect to quinine-sulfate was observed to be around 0.41. The UV Vis spectroscopy of the samples was conducted for checking the absorbance properties.

Unlike common Cu-NPs, instead of having a peak around 500 nm a notch around 400 nm is observed. This notch could be mostly due to the p-p* transition of graphite-like C–C bonds of CDs (Fig. 1 b). The side chains and surface-bound groups could be identified by looking at the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1c). FTIR spectra show peaks around 500-600 cm−1 which could be attributed to the halides present as salt in the samples. The other peaks around 1000, 1200, 1350 cm−1 could be attributed to C–O (OH), C–N and C–H stretching. The major peaks around 1700 cm−1 could be mostly due to C=O groups. It should be also taken into note that in presence of electropositive metal ions nearby cause the IR peaks to shift [29]. The DLS results depicting the hydrodynamic radius of CuCs was observed to be around 100–200 nm (Fig. 1d). The nanostructure of the CuCs was observed using TEM (Fig. 2 a). The Figure suggests the wire-like structure of CuNPs in which CDs were observed to be grafted forming CuCs. The tiny black dots like CDs were clearly depicted (shown by red arrow) in the CuC nanowire like structures (especially in the magnified figure shown inset). The results are in accordance with the DLS results. The SAED pattern matches with copper oxide (JCPDS file no 02–1041) where two major planes [2 0 0] and [2 2 0] was observed to be present in the SAED pattern (Fig. 2b).

The XRD profile was observed to be significantly matching to the TEM results (Fig. 2d). The major angles corresponding to JCPDS file no 02–1041 is observed. Especially the major peaks or angles at 32.41, 35.59, 39.13, 46.53, 49.21, 58.35, 68.43, 72.62° were observed to be from that specific phase.

Even though any patterns for carbon was not observed in SAED, in XRD certain peaks for C8 molecules were observed also (JCPDS 72:2091). The main intensity angles mostly observed in the XRD resulting from CDs are at 29.49, 42.19, 52.31 and 61.19° which is in accordance with our previous report [20]. The bulk structure of the CuCs also clearly understood in FESEM image (Fig. 2c). The structures which were looking like nanoparticles when formed aggregates were observed to form spear like structures. Therefore, formation of copper oxide phases is significantly possible. This result is absolutely in accordance with the TEM results.

From the XPS analysis (Fig. 3 ) it can be conceptualized that during the synthesis a huge proportion of oxygen is incorporated into the system. However, a sharp peak is observed at 932.6 eV proving evidence of metallic copper. A notch at 932.4 eV also proves the presence of Cu–S linkages. However, a hump is also observed around 941.9 ev proving the satellite region for Cu2p3/2 in CuO which suggests the presence of copper oxides in the structure. This also fits with the results obtained from phase analysis of TEM and XRD. Importantly peaks at 167.47 and 168.5 eV also observed which suggests the formation of S–C linkages. However, this does not overlap with carbon disulfide molecule (C–S, 164.1 eV) [30] proving the presence of oxygen molecules which are equivalent to thiol like linkages which might have stabilized the synthesized Cu-nano particles. Major peaks in C1s at 285.84 eV proves the presence of organic like C–O linkage instead of metal carbonates. Around 283.4 eV the strongest peak suggests metal-carbon linkage which proves the concept of CD doping into Cu lattices. At 285 eV the second highest sharp peak provides evidence of C–C linkages of CDs.

The CuCs were observed to be fluorescent. So, its applicability as bioimaging probe was explored using MSCs as a model (Fig. 4 a,b,c). The cells were observed to take fluorescence brightly. Moreover, the dark spot at the center clearly shows that it did not enter the nucleus. Therefore, any risk for interaction with DNA is observed to be minimal. The red region staining with ethidium-bromide was less in number proving higher cellular viability. Therefore, it can be concluded that most of the cells were alive and this probe could be used for live cell imaging. Another important feature observed in Cu-NPs is SERS (Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering) which is basically an enhancement of Raman intensity of the functional groups present bound to a material. In CuC, this effect is observed to be significantly strong (Fig. 4d). The black line basically denotes the Raman spectra of a typical CD sample. It has most of the characteristic features like G and D band of graphite like structures.

The other major strong peaks are due to C=C and C=O groups (1700 cm−1 and 1800 cm−1). Importantly most of these peaks are significantly amplified (5–7 folds) in the spectra of CuCs (red line). The red arrow clearly shows huge amplification of the major peaks from CDs to CuCs with certain shifts due to change in electronic environment due to the presence of strongly electro-active metallic atoms like copper. From XPS, TEM and XRD also we find evidences of CuO phases. Around 1270 cm−1, organometallic carbon copper bonds show specific peaks. Around 1200 cm−1, are also observed. The Raman spectrum was misinterpreted for D band. The D band was observed at 1370 cm−1 (due to edge defects of carbon dots they are shifted from graphitic D band at 1345 cm−1) The overall spectrum intensity was increased 5–6 folds. However, the major enhanced peaks are the G and D bands. The D band shows a red shift due to bonding with a metallic atom whereas G band is blue shifted which could be attributed to surface C–O bonding. This prominently shows the effect of SERS in the CuCs. Therefore, this material could be useful for SERS based sensing and Raman imaging. Along with the TEM, XRD, XPS spectra, the Raman spectroscopy (G and D band, shown in Fig. 4d), results confirm formation and doping of CDs into Cu nanostructures. Further, effective fluorescence microscopy results and SERS signals together support the candidature of CuCs as a multimodal imaging probe.

The MTT results in 24 h were observed to have a lower viability of the samples with respect to the control (Fig. 5 a). It is mostly because of the initial change in cellular microenvironment like changes in osmotic pressure. Although for a higher concentration of CuCs the viability was below 60%, at lower concentration (10 μg/mL) viability was observed to be around 90%. However, the scenario changed drastically in the 72 h study when the viability increased drastically. With lower concentration, the viability reached as high as 350%. Even at higher concentration (30 μg/mL), the viability is more than control. This result proves bioactive nature of the CuCs.

For the in vivo application of CuCs, the material also was taken to cross one more set of experiments. The blood compatibility is one of the major groups of studies that were conducted to check its safety. The hemolysis assay was conducted to check whether CuCs are causing RBCs to burst or not (Fig. 5b).

The hemolysis assay observed to be safe for CuCs. None of the sample concentration crossed more than 5% of hemolysis with respect to Triton-X. The results were observed to be statistically significant in all the concentrations. So, CuCs might not be hemolytic. The cell aggregation studies, however, were not observed to be that much positive (Fig. 5 c, d, and e). The WBCs and platelets were not observed to be aggregated. So, the possibilities of the immune response are low. However, RBCs were observed to get aggregated in some places.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro reactive oxygen species scavenging study
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro ROS scavenging study was conducted to check whether CuCs can scavenge the generated the ROS developed due to light exposure or due to copper ions. Two major radicals were checked for ROS scavenging i.e. superoxides and hydroxyl radicals. For the first one NBT assay was conducted whereas for the other one DCFH-DA assay was conducted. CuCs were observed to lower the superoxide activity with respect to control in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 6 a). However, at higher concentration, there is a rise in superoxide level. This could be mostly as in higher concentration there is a higher amount of Cu2+ ions are involved in the cellular interactions which enhance ROS generation.

In case of hydroxyl radical, however, the scavenging property follows almost a linear pattern with the concentration (Fig. 6b). From low to higher concentration to lower concentration there is a regular change in scavenging activity. Looking at the patterns of the plot it can be concluded that CuCs are better capable of scavenging super oxides than hydroxyl radicals.

The major application of Cu-NPs in biological field is performed mostly due to its antimicrobial properties. To check that property a 100 μg mL−1 CuC suspension was prepared. Standard anti-bacterial and anti-fungal solution (Hi Media) was utilized for this experiment as a control. Two major strains S. aureus and E. coli were used for anti-microbial activity as representative for Gram (+Ve) and Gram (-Ve) family. With respect to control (standard antimicrobial solution) in both strains, samples had lower antimicrobial activity (Fig. 7 a, b). However, in both cases, the activity was more than 70% with respect to the control. The control utilized was antibiotic/antimycotic solution (Hi-Media) in 100X concentration which means it was 100 times stronger than the required dosage. Hence, the antimicrobial activity of the sample is significantly high. For S. aureus, it was more than 90% with respect to control. Most importantly in absolute scale when compared graphically (Fig. 7c), the samples were observed to be effective in generating a similar zone of inhibition for both Gram (+Ve) and Gram (-Ve) strains.

Post in vivo implantation, in rodent models after 7 and 15 days the animals were sacrificed and checked for healing via histological staining. It has been observed that in 7days very small arrays of microvessels have been formed (Fig. 8 a).

In 15 days, study, it was observed that the micro-vessels are taking structures of complete blood vessels (Fig. 8b). No signature of toxicity or necrosis due to exposure of copper was observed. In 15 days, the complete formation of the vasculature with multiple basal layers upon micro vessels development was observed. When masson trichrome staining (MT) was performed even more clear vasculature development was observed under the microscope in 7 days samples (Fig. 8c). Micro vessels are observed to distribute over the wound-bed in three dimensions. In 15 days, study complete vessel formation with thick deposition of collagen and basal layer formation was observed (Fig. 8d).

For further confirmation, immunofluorescence staining for the CD-31 antibody was conducted to the sections to check the presence of endothelial cells. A similar trend of histological staining was observed in case of immunofluorescence staining in 7days where mostly layers of endothelial cells are visible on the microvessels (fig-9a ). The amount of endothelial expression and number of vessel formation is even more prominent in case of 15 days study (fig- 9b). The formation of micro vessels and endothelial lining both are even clearer in this study (marked by white arrow).

The in vitro gene expression studies were conducted to check whether ROS scavenging genes are down-regulated in presence of CuCs and how CuC helps in enhanced endothelial expression (Fig. 10 ). The major genes which were considered in this study are Superoxide Dismutase (SOD), Glutathione Transferase (GT) and NAD-Quinone-Oxidoreductase (NAD) which are the major ROS scavenging enzymes available in the mammalian living systems. In the study, artificially ROS was generated using UV light exposure so that transcription of ROS scavenging genes can take place. In NAD, with respect to control, a clear difference in gene expression is observed in the CuC applied samples. The gene is significantly down-regulated in presence of CuCs. (Fig. 10d). This could be also maybe because of this enzymes’ interaction with bivalent ions such as Cu2+.

However, in case of GT (Fig. 10e), the gene expression is observed to be feeble. Although the similar down-regulation pattern of the gene is observed. Importantly when a higher concentration of CuC was involved, SOD was overexpressed proving that beyond a specific concentration the nanostructure might evoke ROS generation (Fig. 10f). Copper ions are known to generate localized hypoxic microenvironment. Up regulation of HIF-α at lower CuC concentration also proves the similar hypothesis (Fig. 10b). This localized hypoxia might be the reason of overexpression of PECAM (Fig. 10c) which enhances angiogenesis. However, at higher concentration, the results were not observed to be statistically significant. This could be because at higher concentrations CuCs may not be as biocompatible as it is at lower concentrations.

Copper nanoparticles are known to be useful in biomedical applications for its SERS based imaging and antimicrobial properties [13,19]. SERS allows signal amplification with a significantly high signal to noise ratio. This is helpful in imaging and biosensing. Copper also has significant antimicrobial activity against different infection-causing pathogens. There are significant antimicrobial applications of copper nanostructures cited in the literature [31]. It has been used as an antimicrobial agent as bare nanostructures as well as into wound dressing and antimicrobial surfaces [32,33]. Copper also has been observed to enhance angiogenesis via development of the hypoxic environment [34]. This can be helpful in such wounds where lack of angiogenesis and microbial infection impair complete healing [35,36]. However, enhanced copper release affects the red-ox balance of biological system enhancing oxidative stress [37,38]. Our previous study suggests that CDs can scavenge ROS from cellular microenvironment [20]. The plausible mechanism via which therefore doping of CDs can enhance biological acceptability is depicted in Fig. 11 . The Cu2+ ions generated in the system can generate ROS. The generated ROS are taken up by the side chains of CDs and transferred to its carbon core. Further, these structures are emitted into the biological system as carbonate and bicarbonate ions (Fig. 11).

This doping of CDs also affects the biological assay results as well as gene expression results. Therefore, the expression of GT and NAD is downregulated. However, the expression of SOD is observed to be upregulated. This could be interpreted as Cu2+ ions act as metallic cofactor for copper-chaperones which help in activation of SOD [39]. Hence the expression of SOD might be enhanced due to the presence of an excess of copper in the cellular microenvironment. However, the results of other genes, as well as biological assays, suggests that ROS scavenging is effectively performed by doping of CDs. However, the roles of copper ions are not hampered. The SERS activity of copper nanoparticles was observed to be effective via rapid amplification of G and D band structure in Raman spectra. Simultaneously, CuC was observed to be fluorescent and effective in live cell imaging. Hence CuCs can be useful for multimodal imaging. CuCs also observed to provide good antimicrobial activity in comparison to a standard antibiotic and antimycotic solution (HiMedia, 10000 units penicillin, 10 mg streptomycin and 25 μg amphotericin-B per mL of solution) for both Gram-positive as well as Gram-negative bacteria. The gene expression also suggests strong up-regulation of HIF-α and PECAM gene. The gene expression results are further verified via in vivo deep wound model where enhanced endothelial differentiation potential of the material was clearly visible.

With all these above results we can conclude that this material is an effective dual mode imaging probe (fluorescence/SERS). However, besides being a multimodal imaging probe, it can protect cells from ROS induced oxidative stress and therefore might be capable of saving cells from phototoxicity. It also has significant anti-microbial activity. Therefore, it could be useful in developing fluorescent graft or implant coating for in situ monitoring of the intrinsic antimicrobial property. Finally, the gene expression study and animal study prove it helps in enhanced angiogenesis. So, this could be useful for application in doping polymeric composites or hydrogels for rapid deep wound recovery and could be useful for chronic wounds like diabetic foot ulcers and burn injuries where microbial invasions and lack of angiogenesis are the major limitations for recovery and complete healing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge MHRD, Govt of India for awarding the doctoral fellowship to Dr. Bodhisatwa Das.
